intro.title: Let Nox cook
intro.body: A few quick questions so Nox can map what you already know and where to focus. This kicks off your learning world.

presets: Get faster at the math section

confidence_step.prompt: How confident are you with each of these?
confidence_step.note: Self-report only — no wrong answers here. Tap and move on.

probe_step.prompt: Quick check — answer what you can, skip what you can't.

questions.stem: Which of the following is equal to ( n – 3) 2 if n = 11?
questions.skill_gap_if_wrong: Weakness in substitution; exponent meaning; evaluating expressions; recognizing equivalent value vs algebraic identity.
questions.encouragement_hint: Students may think they must expand every expression; may confuse (n−3)^2 with n^2−9; may not realize answer choices can be tested directly.

questions.stem: A square field has an area of 22,500 square feet. To the nearest foot, what is the diagonal distance across the field?
questions.skill_gap_if_wrong: Weakness in square properties; area-to-side inversion; Pythagorean theorem; concept mapping from quadrilaterals to triangles.
questions.encouragement_hint: Students may give the side length instead of the diagonal; may forget to take the square root of area; may not connect square diagonal to right-triangle hypotenuse.

questions.stem: Andrea wants to fill in two sections of her backyard with sod that must be purchased in 2-x-2-foot squares. If the two sections measure 30 x 40 feet and 60 x 80 feet, how many squares of sod does she need to buy?
questions.skill_gap_if_wrong: Weakness in area formulas; unit-square reasoning; multi-step word problem organization; visual diagram use.
questions.encouragement_hint: Students may forget the second section; may divide by 2 instead of 4; may confuse side length of sod square with area; may compute perimeter.

questions.stem: If 5 x + 3 = 10 x – 17, what is the value of x ?
questions.skill_gap_if_wrong: Weakness in like terms; inverse operations; equation balance; fraction operations when coefficients are non-integers.
questions.encouragement_hint: Student may combine unlike terms incorrectly; may make sign mistakes moving terms; may panic if coefficients are fractions even though the process is identical.

questions.stem: When you multiply a number by 4 and then subtract 7, the result is the same as if you first subtracted 7 from the same number and then multiplied by 11. What is the number?
questions.skill_gap_if_wrong: Weakness in verbal translation; parentheses/distributive property; equation setup; interpreting 'same as' as equals.
questions.encouragement_hint: Student may write 11x−7 instead of 11(x−7); may think it is a two-equation system when it is actually one equation from two equivalent expressions; may struggle to define the unknown.

questions.stem: William earned $3,200 per month as a teacher for the ten months from September to June. Then he took a job as a barista at a local café, where he earned $2,000 per month during July and August. What was his average monthly pay for the 12 months?
questions.skill_gap_if_wrong: Weakness in recognizing mean from word problems; weighted average; unit consistency; translating text into math.
questions.encouragement_hint: Student may average the two monthly pay rates directly instead of weighting by number of months; may introduce unnecessary variables; may fail to see it is a mean problem because it is embedded in words.

questions.stem: Which of the following numbers completes the sequence 3, 8, 14, 21, 29, ___?
questions.skill_gap_if_wrong: Weakness in checking first differences; overgeneralizing sequence labels; not distinguishing sequence vs series.
questions.encouragement_hint: Student may assume every sequence question is arithmetic and look for one constant difference. Student may try to connect it to arithmetic/geometric series even though no summation is involved.

questions.stem: Which of the following inequalities is represented by the following number line?
questions.skill_gap_if_wrong: Weakness in number line reading; endpoint inclusion/exclusion; compound inequality notation; interval notation translation.
questions.encouragement_hint: Students may reverse inequality direction, confuse open vs closed circles, treat the interval as absolute value, or forget that compound inequalities need both bounds.

confidence_report.note: Seeded as diagnostic context; self-report should not be recorded as correctness evidence.

diagnostic_complete.note: marks first-login diagnostic done; engine can now serve recommendations.
